_about: §4 of the theory — THE EXCEPTION LEXICON. Some words resist mutation in contexts that would otherwise license it, and the resistance is a fact about the WORD, not its shape: gêm keeps its g- after triggers that soften every native g-word (it is a recent loan); beth, ddoe and dros are FOSSILIZED mutations — historically softened forms that became citation forms and cannot mutate again; dy, pan, mae, mor simply resist (King §12b). This is why Lexeme carries identity (§2): a theory quantifying only over initials could not state these facts. Two kinds of entry: `lexemes` lists individual words; `classes` records class-level generalizations that no finite list can capture — personal names do not mutate in the modern language (i Dafydd, King §12d), non-Welsh placenames resist while Welsh ones mutate (i Birmingham vs i °Fanceinion, §12c — genuinely variable, see contested.json), and an already-mutated initial cannot mutate again (§5c). At judgment time the whole file surfaces as one bit, Lexeme.immutable, whose veto reports counterfactually (§5).
_source: King §12, Appendix A p.489: words/classes resistant to mutation; preposition class per Tallerman (2006 fn. 6) and Ball & Müller (1992: 201). Feeds Lexeme.immutable in the pipeline; theory tests hand-set the flag. Class-level rules are recorded as classes; a closed class with no data signal carries an explicit member list.

lexemes.why: fixed mutation of peth (King §12a)
fixed mutation of doe (King App A)

already-mutated shape of dim carried as a citation form by the broad lexicon (UD lemmatization artifact); a mutated consonant cannot mutate again (King §5c). The live analysis — radical dim, observed SM off the subject edge (King §11a's °ddim) — is untouched: this flag matches only form/lemma ddim

classes.why: personal names do not mutate in the modern language: i Dafydd, i Pedr (King §12d, §36)
i Birmingham; but places with special Welsh names DO mutate (i °Fanceinion), as do Welsh geographical names incl. ll-: i °Landeilo (King §12c, §§37-38); speech variable — CONTESTED (placenames)
a consonant already mutated cannot mutate again (King §5c)

prepositions do not undergo soft mutation synchronically, even in an appropriate environment (Tallerman 2006 fn. 6, citing Ball & Müller 1992: 201); King's practice agrees — gyda is never marked mutated in 70 occurrences, including directly after NP edges. The forcing datum is the possession construction, Mae rhywbeth arall gyda fi: the subject NP's edge would otherwise license synt:xp-edge SM on gyda. The historical d-doublets (dan/tan, dros/tros, drwy/trwy — King §12a on dros) froze in the earlier phonological-mutation era and are radical lexemes now. No data layer carries prepositionhood (extraction is open-class only), so this closed class is operationalized by the member list; dotted keys name the prepositional homograph (yn.loc, cyn.prep).
